findings.description: login builds the SQL query with an f-string interpolating the caller-supplied email, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. authentication bypass or data exfiltration via crafted email).
validate_session has the expiry comparison inverted: unexpired (valid) sessions are deleted and rejected as expired, while genuinely expired sessions fall through and return user_id as valid.
Because rotate_token trusts the inverted validate_session, an expired token can be exchanged for a fresh 12-hour session indefinitely (and rotating a valid token destroys it and returns None).
Account lockout never expires or resets with time (no lockout timestamp, LOCKOUT_MINUTES never applied), so 5 bad attempts permanently lock any known email -- a trivial targeted denial-of-service.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations is well below the current OWASP recommendation (600,000), weakening offline cracking resistance of stored hashes.
find_user_by_email runs SELECT * FROM users with fetchall and filters in Python, loading and materializing the entire users table per lookup instead of using WHERE email = ? with a parameter.
Unknown-email logins return before any PBKDF2 work while known emails incur ~100k hash iterations, creating a timing side channel usable for user enumeration.
Sessions live only in a per-process in-memory dict, so all sessions are lost on restart and are invisible to other worker processes, breaking validation in any multi-process deployment.
_sessions is mutated without any locking; in a threaded server cleanup_expired's snapshot-then-delete loop can race with validate_session/logout and raise KeyError or lose updates.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated (Python 3.12+) and yields naive timestamps; datetime.now(timezone.utc) is the safe replacement to avoid future removal and naive/aware comparison hazards.
Email matching is case-sensitive and unnormalized in both the SQL lookup (line 57) and the Python comparison, so User@x.com and user@x.com are treated as different accounts.
find_user_by_email hands back the full User record including password_hash and salt, spreading sensitive credential material to any calling layer that only needs identity fields.
Expired sessions accumulate in _sessions unboundedly unless an external caller remembers to invoke cleanup_expired, a slow memory leak under churn.

login returns None for unknown email, locked account, and wrong password alike, so callers cannot tell a locked user to wait rather than retry (possibly deliberate anti-enumeration, hence low confidence).
LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but never referenced anywhere in the module (dead constant tied to the missing lockout-expiry logic).
Mixed typing styles: typing.Optional is imported while PEP 585 builtin generics (dict[str, Session]) are used elsewhere; X | None would be consistent on the required Python version.
